{{Short description|Browser game released in 2018}}{{Infobox video game
| title = Bad News
| image = 
| caption = 
| developer = DROG and [[University of Cambridge]]
| platforms = [[Web browser|Browser]]
| released = February 19, 2018
| designer = Gusmanson
}}
'''''Bad News''''' is a free [[browser game]] in which players take the perspective of a fake news [[tycoon]]. It was released on February 19, 2018. The game is classified as a [[serious game]] and a [[newsgame]] aimed at improving media literacy and social impact.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aboutbadnews.com/social-impact-game|title=Social Impact Game|date=5 April 2018|access-date=30 July 2020|archive-date=10 May 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180510050641/https://www.aboutbadnews.com/social-impact-game|url-status=dead}}</ref> The game was produced by the Dutch media platform "DROG"<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aboutbadnews.com/|title=About Bad News|date=5 April 2018|access-date=30 July 2020|archive-date=20 February 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180220054948/https://www.aboutbadnews.com/|url-status=dead}}</ref> in collaboration with [[University of Cambridge]] scientists. The game has been described by the media as a "fake news vaccine".<ref>{{cite web| url =https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-43154667| title =Game helps players spot 'fake news'| publisher =BBC News| date =22 February 2018| access-date =30 July 2020| archive-date =29 July 2020| archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200729104302/https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-43154667| url-status =live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web| url =https://www.technologyreview.com/the-download/610320/this-video-game-wants-to-be-a-fake-news-vaccine/| title =This video game wants to be a fake news vaccine| publisher =MIT Technology Review| date =20 February 2018| access-date =30 July 2020| archive-date =9 November 2018| archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20181109051543/https://www.technologyreview.com/the-download/610320/this-video-game-wants-to-be-a-fake-news-vaccine/| url-status =live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web| url =https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2018/feb/20/bad-news-the-game-researchers-hope-will-vaccinate-public-against-fake-news| title=Bad News: the game researchers hope will 'vaccinate' public against fake news| work = The Guardian | date = 19 February 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite web| url = https://edition.cnn.com/2019/07/04/media/fake-news-game-vaccine/index.html| title = Researchers have created a 'vaccine' for fake news. It's a game| publisher = CNN| date = 4 July 2019| access-date = 30 July 2020| archive-date = 18 January 2026| archive-url = https://archive.today/20260118085412/https://edition.cnn.com/2019/07/04/media/fake-news-game-vaccine/index.html| url-status = live}}</ref>

==Gameplay==
The purpose of the game is to immunize the public against misinformation by letting players take on the role of a fake news producer. In the game, players earn "badges",<ref>{{cite web| url =https://www.reuters.com/video/2018/03/20/fake-news-vaccine-teaches-you-to-spot-di?videoId=410596269| title=Fake news 'vaccine' teaches you to spot disinformation| publisher = Reuters| date = 20 March 2018}}</ref> each of which correspond to common techniques used in the production of fake news, such as [[Political polarization|polarization]], [[conspiracy theories]], [[discrediting]], [[trolling]], and invoking [[emotion]]. The goal is to build your own fake news empire and gain as many followers as possible while maintaining credibility. The game is based on a psychological concept known as [[inoculation theory]], where exposure to weakened doses of common fake news tactics is thought to help confer resistance or psychological immunity against fake news.<ref>{{cite web| url =https://www.npr.org/2018/03/18/594671289/spot-fake-news-by-making-it| title =Spot Fake News By Making It| publisher =NPR| date =19 March 2018| access-date =30 July 2020| archive-date =30 October 2020| archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20201030174508/https://www.npr.org/2018/03/18/594671289/spot-fake-news-by-making-it| url-status =live}}</ref>

==Reception==
The game received the 2020 Brouwers Trust Prize from the [[Koninklijke Hollandsche Maatschappij der Wetenschappen|Royal Holland Society of Sciences]]<ref>{{cite web| url = https://khmw.nl/brouwer-vertrouwensprijs-2020-online-game-tegen-fake-news-wint-100-000-euro-voor-verdere-ontwikkeling/| publisher = Koninklijke Hollandsche Maatschappij der Wetenschappen| title=Brouwer Vertrouwensprijs 2020: Online game tegen fake news wint 100.000 euro voor verdere ontwikkeling| date = 16 January 2020}}</ref> and the [[Frank Prize]] in Public Interest Communications from the [[University of Florida]]'s College of Journalism.<ref>{{cite web| url = https://realgoodcenter.jou.ufl.edu/2020-research-prize-in-public-interest-communications/| publisher = University of Florida| title = 2020 Research Prize in Public Interest Communications| date = 16 January 2020| access-date = 30 July 2020| archive-date = 6 August 2020| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20200806153141/https://realgoodcenter.jou.ufl.edu/2020-research-prize-in-public-interest-communications/| url-status = dead}}</ref>

The game was on exhibit in the London [[Design Museum]] for Beazley's Designs of the Year<ref>{{cite web| url = https://www.beazley.com/Documents/Beazley%20DotY%20final.pdf| publisher = Design Museum| title = Fenty Beauty by Rihanna, the world's first plastic-free shopping aisle, and the SpaceX Falcon Heavyrocket: Design Museum announces Beazley Designs of the Year nominees| date = 2018| access-date = 2020-07-30| archive-date = 2021-03-03| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20210303140743/https://www.beazley.com/Documents/Beazley%20DotY%20final.pdf| url-status = dead}}</ref> and the [[University of South Australia]]'s Museum of Discovery<ref>{{cite web| url = https://mod.org.au/exhibits/bad-news-game/| publisher = Museum of Discovery and Science| title = Bad News Game| date = 2020| access-date = 2020-07-30| archive-date = 2020-09-19| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20200919231853/https://mod.org.au/exhibits/bad-news-game/| url-status = live}}</ref> and has been the subject of scientific research evaluating its impact on [[fake news]] discernment and recognition.<ref name="Roozenbeek">{{cite journal | last1 = Roozenbeek| first1 = J | last2 = van der Linden| first2 = S |year = 2018| title =  The fake news game: actively inoculating against the risk of misinformation | journal = Journal of Risk Research | volume = 22 | issue = 5| pages = 570–580 | url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13669877.2018.1443491|doi = 10.1080/13669877.2018.1443491| s2cid = 149129860 | url-access = subscription }}</ref><ref name="Roozenbeek2">{{cite journal|last1=Roozenbeek|first1=J|last2=van der Linden|first2=S|year=2019|title=Fake news game confers psychological resistance against online misinformation|journal=Palgrave Communications|volume=5|issue=65|doi=10.1057/s41599-019-0279-9|s2cid=195329457|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="Roozenbeek3">{{cite journal | last1 = Basol| first1 = M | last2 = Roozenbeek| first2 = J | last3 = van der Linden| first3 = S |year = 2020| title = Good News about Bad News: Gamified Inoculation Boosts Confidence and Cognitive Immunity Against Fake News| journal = Journal of Cognition | volume = 3 | issue = 1 | page = 2 |  doi = 10.5334/joc.91| pmid = 31934684 | pmc = 6952868 | doi-access = free}}</ref><ref name="Roozenbeek4">{{cite journal | last1 = Roozenbeek| first1 = J | last2 = van der Linden| first2 = S | last3 = Nygren| first3 = T |year = 2020| title = Prebunking interventions based on "inoculation" theory can reduce susceptibility to misinformation across cultures| url = https://misinforeview.hks.harvard.edu/article/global-vaccination-badnews/| journal = Harvard Kennedy School Misinformation Review | volume = 1 | issue = 2}} {{doi|10.37016//mr-2020-008|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="Roozenbeek5">{{cite journal | last1 = Roozenbeek| first1 = J | last2 = Maertens| first2 = R | last3 = McClanahan| first3 = P | last4 = van der Linden | first4 = S | year = 2020| title = Disentangling Item and Testing Effects in Inoculation Research on Online Misinformation: Solomon Revisited| journal = Educational and Psychological Measurement | volume = 81 | issue = 2 | pages = 340–362 | doi = 10.1177/0013164420940378| doi-access = free| pmid = 37929261 | pmc = 10621688 }}</ref>

==Localization==
In 2020, [[Video game localization|localised versions]] of the game were released for Eastern European markets where [[Russian disinformation in the post-Soviet era|Russian-backed disinformation]] is of particular concern. [[Debunk.org]] published multiple-language versions of the game in Russian, Lithuanian, Latvian, Estonian and Polish.<ref>{{cite web| url =https://www.delfi.lt/en/lifestyle/recognize-disinformation-after-this-game-you-will-be-expert-in-recognising-fake-news.d?id=85216601| title =Recognize disinformation: after this game, you will be expert in recognising fake news| publisher =[[Delfi (web portal)|Delfi]]| date =11 September 2020| access-date =28 February 2023| archive-date =28 February 2023| archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20230228145845/https://www.delfi.lt/en/lifestyle/recognize-disinformation-after-this-game-you-will-be-expert-in-recognising-fake-news.d?id=85216601| url-status =live}}</ref>
